lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Set down, set down your honourable load,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: If honour may be shrouded in a hearse,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Whilst I awhile obsequiously lament

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: The untimely fall of virtuous Lancaster.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Poor key-cold figure of a holy king!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Pale ashes of the house of Lancaster!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Thou bloodless remnant of that royal blood!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Be it lawful that I invocate thy ghost,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: To hear the lamentations of poor Anne,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Wife to thy Edward, to thy slaughter’d son,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Stabb’d by the selfsame hand that made these wounds!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Lo, in these windows that let forth thy life,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: I pour the helpless balm of my poor eyes.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Cursed be the hand that made these fatal holes!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Cursed be the heart that had the heart to do it!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Cursed the blood that let this blood from hence!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: More direful hap betide that hated wretch,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: That makes us wretched by the death of thee,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Than I can wish to adders, spiders, toads,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Or any creeping venom’d thing that lives!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: If ever he have child, abortive be it,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Prodigious, and untimely brought to light,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Whose ugly and unnatural aspect

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: May fright the hopeful mother at the view;

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And that be heir to his unhappiness!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: If ever he have wife, let her be made

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: As miserable by the death of him

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: As I am made by my poor lord and thee!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Come, now towards Chertsey with your holy load,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Taken from Paul’s to be interred there;

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And still, as you are weary of the weight,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Rest you, whiles I lament King Henry’s corse.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Stay, you that bear the corse, and set it down.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: What black magician conjures up this fiend,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: To stop devoted charitable deeds?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Villains, set down the corse; or, by Saint Paul,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I’ll make a corse of him that disobeys.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: My lord, stand back, and let the coffin pass.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Unmanner’d dog! stand thou, when I command:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Advance thy halberd higher than my breast,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Or, by Saint Paul, I’ll strike thee to my foot,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And spurn upon thee, beggar, for thy boldness.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: What, do you tremble? are you all afraid?

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Alas, I blame you not; for you are mortal,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And mortal eyes cannot endure the devil.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Avaunt, thou dreadful minister of hell!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Thou hadst but power over his mortal body,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: His soul thou canst not have; therefore, be gone.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Sweet saint, for charity, be not so curst.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Foul devil, for God’s sake, hence, and trouble us not;

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: For thou hast made the happy earth thy hell,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Fill’d it with cursing cries and deep exclaims.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: If thou delight to view thy heinous deeds,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Behold this pattern of thy butcheries.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: O, gentlemen, see, see! dead Henry’s wounds

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Open their congeal’d mouths and bleed afresh!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Blush, blush, thou lump of foul deformity;

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: For ’tis thy presence that exhales this blood

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: From cold and empty veins, where no blood dwells;

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Thy deed, inhuman and unnatural,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Provokes this deluge most unnatural.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: O God, which this blood madest, revenge his death!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: O earth, which this blood drink’st revenge his death!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Either heaven with lightning strike the murderer dead,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Or earth, gape open wide and eat him quick,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: As thou dost swallow up this good king’s blood,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Which his hell-govern’d arm hath butchered!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Lady, you know no rules of charity,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which renders good for bad, blessings for curses.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Villain, thou know’st no law of God nor man:

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: No beast so fierce but knows some touch of pity.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But I know none, and therefore am no beast.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: O wonderful, when devils tell the truth!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: More wonderful, when angels are so angry.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Vouchsafe, divine perfection of a woman,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Of these supposed evils, to give me leave,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: By circumstance, but to acquit myself.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Vouchsafe, defused infection of a man,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: For these known evils, but to give me leave,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: By circumstance, to curse thy cursed self.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Fairer than tongue can name thee, let me have

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Some patient leisure to excuse myself.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Fouler than heart can think thee, thou canst make

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: No excuse current, but to hang thyself.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: By such despair, I should accuse myself.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And, by despairing, shouldst thou stand excused;

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: For doing worthy vengeance on thyself,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Which didst unworthy slaughter upon others.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Say that I slew them not?

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Why, then they are not dead:

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: But dead they are, and, devilish slave, by thee.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I did not kill your husband.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Why, then he is alive.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Nay, he is dead; and slain by Edward’s hand.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: In thy foul throat thou liest: Queen Margaret saw

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Thy murderous falchion smoking in his blood;

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: The which thou once didst bend against her breast,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: But that thy brothers beat aside the point.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I was provoked by her slanderous tongue,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which laid their guilt upon my guiltless shoulders.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Thou wast provoked by thy bloody mind,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Which never dreamt on aught but butcheries:

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Didst thou not kill this king?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I grant ye.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Dost grant me, hedgehog? then, God grant me too

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Thou mayst be damned for that wicked deed!

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: O, he was gentle, mild, and virtuous!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The fitter for the King of heaven, that hath him.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: He is in heaven, where thou shalt never come.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Let him thank me, that holp to send him thither;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: For he was fitter for that place than earth.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: And thou unfit for any place but hell.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Yes, one place else, if you will hear me name it.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Some dungeon.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Your bed-chamber.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Ill rest betide the chamber where thou liest!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: So will it, madam till I lie with you.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: I hope so.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I know so. But, gentle Lady Anne,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To leave this keen encounter of our wits,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And fall somewhat into a slower method,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Is not the causer of the timeless deaths

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Of these Plantagenets, Henry and Edward,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: As blameful as the executioner?

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Thou art the cause, and most accursed effect.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Your beauty was the cause of that effect;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Your beauty, which did haunt me in my sleep

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To undertake the death of all the world,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: So I might live one hour in your sweet bosom.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: If I thought that, I tell thee, homicide,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: These nails should rend that beauty from my cheeks.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: These eyes could never endure sweet beauty’s wreck;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: You should not blemish it, if I stood by:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: As all the world is cheered by the sun,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: So I by that; it is my day, my life.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Black night o’ershade thy day, and death thy life!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Curse not thyself, fair creature; thou art both.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: I would I were, to be revenged on thee.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: It is a quarrel most unnatural,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To be revenged on him that loveth you.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: It is a quarrel just and reasonable,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: To be revenged on him that slew my husband.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He that bereft thee, lady, of thy husband,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Did it to help thee to a better husband.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: His better doth not breathe upon the earth.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: He lives that loves thee better than he could.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Name him.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Plantagenet.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Why, that was he.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The selfsame name, but one of better nature.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Where is he?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Here. She spitteth at him. Why dost thou spit at me?

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Would it were mortal poison, for thy sake!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Never came poison from so sweet a place.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Never hung poison on a fouler toad.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Out of my sight! thou dost infect my eyes.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Thine eyes, sweet lady, have infected mine.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Would they were basilisks, to strike thee dead!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I would they were, that I might die at once;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: For now they kill me with a living death.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Those eyes of thine from mine have drawn salt tears,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Shamed their aspect with store of childish drops:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: These eyes, which never shed remorseful tear,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: No, when my father York and Edward wept,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To hear the piteous moan that Rutland made

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: When black-faced Clifford shook his sword at him;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Nor when thy warlike father, like a child,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Told the sad story of my father’s death,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And twenty times made pause to sob and weep,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That all the standers-by had wet their cheeks,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Like trees bedash’d with rain: in that sad time

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My manly eyes did scorn an humble tear;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And what these sorrows could not thence exhale,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Thy beauty hath, and made them blind with weeping.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I never sued to friend nor enemy;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My tongue could never learn sweet smoothing words;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But, now thy beauty is proposed my fee,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My proud heart sues, and prompts my tongue to speak. She looks scornfully at him.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Teach not thy lips such scorn, for they were made

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: For kissing, lady, not for such contempt.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: If thy revengeful heart cannot forgive,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Lo, here I lend thee this sharp-pointed sword;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Which if thou please to hide in this true bosom,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And let the soul forth that adoreth thee,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I lay it naked to the deadly stroke,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And humbly beg the death upon my knee. He lays his breast open: she offers at it with his sword.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Nay, do not pause; for I did kill King Henry,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But ’twas thy beauty that provoked me.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Nay, now dispatch; ’twas I that stabb’d young Edward,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But ’twas thy heavenly face that set me on. Here she lets fall the sword.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Take up the sword again, or take up me.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Arise, dissembler: though I wish thy death,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: I will not be the executioner.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Then bid me kill myself, and I will do it.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: I have already.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Tush, that was in thy rage:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Speak it again, and, even with the word,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That hand, which, for thy love, did kill thy love,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Shall, for thy love, kill a far truer love;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To both their deaths thou shalt be accessary.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: I would I knew thy heart.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: ’Tis figured in my tongue.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: I fear me both are false.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Then never man was true.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Well, well, put up your sword.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Say, then, my peace is made.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: That shall you know hereafter.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But shall I live in hope?

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: All men, I hope, live so.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Vouchsafe to wear this ring.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: To take is not to give.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Look, how this ring encompasseth thy finger,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Even so thy breast encloseth my poor heart;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Wear both of them, for both of them are thine.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And if thy poor devoted suppliant may

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But beg one favour at thy gracious hand,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Thou dost confirm his happiness for ever.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: What is it?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That it would please thee leave these sad designs

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To him that hath more cause to be a mourner,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And presently repair to Crosby Place;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Where, after I have solemnly interr’d

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: At Chertsey monastery this noble king,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And wet his grave with my repentant tears,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I will with all expedient duty see you:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: For divers unknown reasons, I beseech you,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Grant me this boon.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: With all my heart; and much it joys me too,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: To see you are become so penitent.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Tressel and Berkeley, go along with me.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Bid me farewell.

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: ’Tis more than you deserve;

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: But since you teach me how to flatter you,

lines.speaker: ANNE
lines.text: Imagine I have said farewell already.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Sirs, take up the corse.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Towards Chertsey, noble lord?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: No, to White-Friars; there attend my coming.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Was ever woman in this humour woo’d?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Was ever woman in this humour won?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I’ll have her; but I will not keep her long.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: What! I, that kill’d her husband and his father,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To take her in her heart’s extremest hate,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: With curses in her mouth, tears in her eyes,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The bleeding witness of her hatred by;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Having God, her conscience, and these bars against me,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And I nothing to back my suit at all,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But the plain devil and dissembling looks,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And yet to win her, all the world to nothing!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Ha!

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Hath she forgot already that brave prince,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Edward, her lord, whom I, some three months since,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Stabb’d in my angry mood at Tewksbury?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: A sweeter and a lovelier gentleman,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Framed in the prodigality of nature,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Young, valiant, wise, and, no doubt, right royal,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: The spacious world cannot again afford:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And will she yet debase her eyes on me,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That cropp’d the golden prime of this sweet prince,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And made her widow to a woeful bed?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: On me, whose all not equals Edward’s moiety?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: On me, that halt and am unshapen thus?

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My dukedom to a beggarly denier,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I do mistake my person all this while:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Upon my life, she finds, although I cannot,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Myself to be a marvellous proper man.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I’ll be at charges for a looking-glass,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And entertain some score or two of tailors,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: To study fashions to adorn my body:

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Since I am crept in favour with myself,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: I will maintain it with some little cost.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: But first I’ll turn yon fellow in his grave;

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: And then return lamenting to my love.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: Shine out, fair sun, till I have bought a glass,

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: That I may see my shadow as I pass.

stage_directions.text: Enter the corpse of King Henry the Sixth, Gentlemen with halberds to guard it; Lady Anne being the mourner.
Exeunt Lady Anne, Tressel, and Berkeley.

glosses.definition: a framework for a coffin; here, the funeral bier
in a manner befitting obsequies (funeral rites) — solemnly, not the modern sense of fawning
Henry VI, head of the Lancastrian royal house; Anne calls him 'virtuous' against Yorkist propaganda
cold as metal keys — proverbial for the chill of a corpse
pale remains; the dead body addressed as the ash of a burnt-out fire
call upon thy spirit — a formal necromantic address
Henry VI's son Edward of Lancaster, Anne's first husband — killed by Richard at Tewkesbury

glosses.phrase: windows that let forth thy life
glosses.definition: the wounds through which his life departed
tears that cannot heal
may a dreadful fate fall upon
conventional poisonous/cursed creatures of Elizabethan imprecation
deformed at birth; monstrously misshapen
portentously monstrous — a prodigy of nature, in the old sense of ominous freak
Chertsey Abbey, west of London — historical burial site of Henry VI
St Paul's Cathedral; the funeral procession has passed through
corpse
dark sorcerer — Anne's first sight of Richard casts him as a conjurer
a long-handled axe-and-spear, carried by guards
begone; away (archaic command to depart)
awful agent of hell — minister = servant
loud lamentations

glosses.phrase: Open their congeal’d mouths and bleed afresh
glosses.definition: the folk belief that a corpse's wounds bleed again in the murderer's presence — cruentation, admitted as legal evidence in some Elizabethan trials
draws out; calls forth (from cold veins)
alive (as in 'the quick and the dead')
arm ruled by hell — Anne's accusation that Richard's body is Satan's instrument
neither divine nor human law
deign, condescend to grant
shapeless contagion — 'defused' = disordered; the word twists Gloucester's 'divine perfection'
Anne's bitter sentence: the only valid excuse is suicide
in Christian theology, the sin against the Holy Spirit that precludes repentance — a loaded word here and throughout the play, esp. 5.3
a curved broadsword
prickly, hostile creature — Anne's epithet for Richard, whose heraldic badge was the white boar (similarly bristled)
helped (archaic past tense of 'help')
sharp verbal fencing — Richard explicitly names the scene's mode of stichomythic dueling
untimely deaths — deaths outside their proper time
the royal house of Plantagenet — Henry VI and his son Edward both bore the name
Gloucester's cynical self-offer as replacement husband
Gloucester names himself by the dynastic family name rather than a personal one — claiming royal continuity
stage direction — Anne's physical refusal
legendary serpents whose gaze killed; Anne wishes her eyes were weapons
the paradox of being dead in life — a lover's commonplace Richard weaponizes
the Lancastrian lord who killed young Rutland (York's youngest son) at Wakefield — 'black-faced' for his brutality; referenced by name in 3 Henry VI
spattered with rain — Gloucester's image of weeping trees
flattering, conciliatory speech
Richard's offered weapon — the scene's bravura stage image: he kneels, bares his chest
stage direction — the theatrical peak; Anne holds Richard's sword at his exposed chest

glosses.phrase: heavenly face that set me on
glosses.definition: Richard claims her beauty drove him to murder her husband — the impossible seduction's rhetorical peak
deceiver, hypocrite — Anne calls him by what he is even as she yields
deign to wear this ring — Richard's formal suit-token
Richard's London residence in Bishopsgate — a real building, parts of which survived into the 20th century
the same Chertsey Abbey named earlier; Richard promises to complete the interment

glosses.phrase: Was ever woman in this humour woo’d
glosses.definition: was ever woman courted in these conditions — Richard's self-astonished theatrical punctuation of the scene; a soliloquy set-piece
the 1471 battle where Prince Edward of Lancaster was killed, consolidating Yorkist power
nature's extravagant generosity — Prince Edward was lavishly endowed
cut short the golden youth — the mowing-down of young life
half of Edward's worth — Richard asks how she could accept a fraction of her first husband
limp and am deformed — Richard returns to his signature self-description
I'll bet my dukedom against a farthing (denier = the least French coin)
a marvelously handsome man — Richard's sarcastic self-estimate
a sardonic closing apostrophe; Richard addresses the sun (echoing 'sun of York') as he goes to buy a mirror

annotations.title: The premise as bravura
annotations.body: Harold Bloom called this "the greatest scene in Shakespeare between two people," and the claim rests on a simple observation: the premise is impossible. Anne enters with the corpse of her father-in-law Henry VI, whom Richard helped kill; her husband Edward, whom Richard also killed, is freshly dead. Richard interrupts her procession, openly admits both murders, offers her his sword against his bare chest, and leaves the scene engaged to her. No naturalistic logic supports the scene; Shakespeare makes it work anyway, through sheer rhetorical velocity. The wooing compresses what in most seductions would take acts or lifetimes into one uninterrupted span of stichomythia (line-for-line exchange) and bravura self-offering. It is the play's first demonstration that Richard's power is linguistic before it is political. What can be said in English he can make happen.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: Stichomythia and the duel of wits
annotations.body: The central exchange between Richard and Anne is a virtuoso deployment of stichomythia — the classical-dramatic convention of alternating single-line retorts, inherited from Seneca. Each speaker seizes the opponent's diction and inverts it: "Vouchsafe, divine perfection of a woman" becomes "Vouchsafe, defused infection of a man"; "More wonderful, when angels are so angry" answers "O wonderful, when devils tell the truth." This is not decorative. In a play whose hero is defined by his mastery of language, stichomythia is the duel's genre, and the scene's dramatic stakes are whether Anne can sustain her rhetorical position. She loses by degrees — first when she accepts the term of the exchange, then when she repurposes her own curse ("despair, and die") against Richard without noticing how he has already begun to convert it. The scene charts defeat by rhetoric.
sources: Brian Vickers, The Artistry of Shakespeare's Prose (1968)

annotations.title: Anne's psychology, or: the seduction is not simple
annotations.body: It is tempting to read Anne as simply outmanoeuvred, but her yielding is more complex than defeat. She is young, widowed, exhausted, surrounded by the murderers of her family; the Yorkists have won the war, and the England she knew as Prince Edward's wife no longer exists. Richard's offered sword at his bared chest is the scene's theatrical peak, but the more telling moment is the line she lets fall: "Arise, dissembler: though I wish thy death, / I will not be the executioner." She names him accurately — dissembler — and withholds violence from him anyway. This is not straightforward seduction; it is the capitulation of a person who has run out of allies, register by register. Contemporary critics (Janet Adelman, Madonne Miner) have resisted the older reading of Anne as dupe or hysteric, arguing instead that her exhaustion is the point, and that Richard's triumph depends on a specific kind of despair that the wars have produced in the women who survived.
sources: Madonne M. Miner, "'Neither mother, wife, nor England's queen'" (1980)

annotations.title: "Was ever woman in this humour woo'd?" as theatrical punctuation
annotations.body: After Anne exits, Richard holds the stage alone and delivers one of Shakespeare's most audacious soliloquies. "Was ever woman in this humour woo'd? / Was ever woman in this humour won?" The rhetorical questions function as theatrical punctuation — Richard makes the audience acknowledge what we have just witnessed. He then surveys the impossibility: I killed her husband, I killed his father, she saw me with their blood on me, and still she accepted my ring. The soliloquy is the scene's control panel; Richard explains to us how outrageous it has been, and thereby forecloses any naturalistic objection we might raise. He is also, crucially, beginning to mock his own power: the last lines ("I'll be at charges for a looking-glass...") shade into comic preening. Richard cannot sustain anyone's devotion, including his own. What he can do is speak anything into being — for as long as he keeps speaking.
sources: A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)
Emrys Jones, Scenic Form in Shakespeare (1971)

annotations.title: The biographical scaffolding Richard invokes
annotations.body: Richard's claim that Anne's father Warwick ("thy warlike father") once wept over the death of Richard's own father, the Duke of York — killed at Wakefield in 1460 by Margaret's forces — is not Shakespeare's invention. The detail comes from Holinshed and ultimately from More. Rutland, the young son York and Clarence's biological brother, was killed at Wakefield by Clifford; Richard here weaponizes the family's grief-record to claim emotional cover. The moment illuminates something important about the tetralogy: Richard III is the final play in Shakespeare's first historical sequence (1, 2, 3 Henry VI and Richard III), and many of its scenes assume the audience's memory of the prior plays. Shakespeare trusts his audience to recall who Rutland was, who Clifford was, and what Wakefield cost. Readers encountering Richard III on its own lose some of this density; editions often footnote the prior-play references for that reason.
sources: E. M. W. Tillyard, Shakespeare's History Plays (1944)

annotations.title: The ring — prop and symbol
annotations.body: Richard's gift of the ring — "Vouchsafe to wear this ring" — is one of the scene's two decisive physical gestures (the other is the sword-on-chest offer). Theatrically the ring does heavy work. It converts rhetorical promise into material token; it stands in for consent Anne has not quite spoken; and it will haunt Anne's later remorse (4.1) when she recalls this moment as a curse self-inflicted. Productions stage the ring variously: placed on Anne's finger against her will, taken from her listless hand, left by Richard at her feet. None of the choices are wrong, and all carry different readings of what yielding means here. The line "To take is not to give" is Anne's attempt to hold a boundary; the stage action that follows is where directors and actors decide what she has actually done.
sources: John Russell Brown, Shakespeare's Plays in Performance (1966)
Alan C. Dessen and Leslie Thomson, A Dictionary of Stage Directions in English Drama 1580-1642 (1999)

annotations.title: "Myself to be a marvellous proper man"
annotations.body: The scene's closing joke — Richard decides the wooing's success must mean he is, after all, handsome — is where the play first hints at Richard's self-division. He has convinced Anne with a performance he does not himself believe; the joke depends on the audience's knowledge that the performance is artificial. But Richard partly believes it too, or wants to. The line "I'll be at charges for a looking-glass / And entertain some score or two of tailors" is comic preening that also reveals appetite. This is the fissure the ghosts will eventually widen in 5.3: Richard is a villain who still wants to be loved. The scene leaves him laughing at Anne's gullibility and at his own power, but the closing apostrophe — "Shine out, fair sun, till I have bought a glass" — addresses the sun he mocked in 1.1's opening line. He has begun to flatter his own creation.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

trials.prompt: Whose corpse does Anne accompany at the scene's opening?
options: Her husband Edward, Prince of Wales
Her father Warwick "the Kingmaker"
Her father-in-law Henry VI, the former Lancastrian king
Richard III's father, the Duke of York

trials.prompt: What is stichomythia, the rhetorical device that structures Anne and Richard's central exchange?
options: Extended metaphor sustained across multiple speakers
Alternating single-line retorts in which each speaker seizes and inverts the opponent's diction
A formal debate with a moderator between disputants
Rhyming couplets passed between two characters

trials.prompt: Which reading of Anne's "yielding" best reflects twentieth- and twenty-first-century scholarship on the scene?
options: She is an unrealistic character whom Shakespeare could not render plausibly
She is straightforwardly seduced by Richard's superior wit
Her yielding is the capitulation of a widowed, surrounded, exhausted woman in a conquered political world — more complex than defeat
She is engineered by Margaret's curse and has no agency in the scene

trials.prompt: When Richard offers Anne his bare chest and his own sword, inviting her to kill him, what is the rhetorical logic?
options: Genuine self-sacrifice to atone for his crimes
A piece of theatre that forces Anne to either strike (implausible) or release him (inevitable), converting her hatred into reluctant mercy
A religious gesture invoking martyrdom as penance
A legal offering: the sword represents the authority she would need to judge him

trials.prompt: Richard's closing couplet — "Was ever woman in this humour woo'd? / Was ever woman in this humour won?" — functions how in the scene's structure?
options: As a genuine expression of self-doubt and bewilderment
As theatrical punctuation: he makes the audience acknowledge the impossibility of what we have just witnessed
As a conventional prayer of thanksgiving
As a formal vow to remain faithful to Anne

trials.prompt: In the soliloquy after Anne exits, Richard's final joke — that her acceptance must mean he is "a marvellous proper man" — suggests what about his psychology?
options: He has sincerely fallen in love with her
The play will not treat him psychologically; he is a flat villain
He is self-divided: a villain who has begun to flatter his own performance and wants, partly, to be loved
He has realized Anne will be a political liability

trials.prompt: Richard tells Anne that her father Warwick once wept over the death of Richard's own father, the Duke of York. This reference draws on what source material?
options: Shakespeare's invention for the scene
Plutarch's Lives, via North's 1579 translation
The earlier plays 1, 2, and 3 Henry VI (and their chronicle sources) — the audience is expected to remember Wakefield and Rutland
A fictional backstory with no prior reference

trials.prompt: What is the larger argument the scene makes about Richard's power?
options: His power is primarily military
His power is primarily financial
His power is linguistic before it is political — what he can say he can make happen
His power depends on supernatural agency, not rhetoric
